sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE, SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 58, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 57 of about 71 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Nothing changed! After SPACE x2, the state is identical to before:
- White at cols 36-40 (16 pixels)
- Green at cols 41-43
- Center blue center at cols 33-34 - still inside the frame
So pressing SPACE twice (retract then extend) didn't collect the center blue center. The state is unchanged.
Let me reconsider the mechanic. Maybe the issue is that the white extension needs to be pointing TOWARD the frame, not away from it.
Currently:
- White at cols 36-40 (left side of green)
- Green at cols 41-43
- Frame at cols 32-35 (to the LEFT of the player)
- White is on the LEFT side of the green, pointing LEFT toward the frame
So the white IS pointing toward the frame. But SPACE didn't collect the blue center.
Wait, let me re-examine the earlier successful collections more carefully.
Right blue square:
- Player at cols 40-43 (green at 40-42, white at 43)
- Frame at cols 44-47
- White is on the RIGHT side of the green, pointing RIGHT toward the frame
- SPACE extended white from col 43 to cols 43-47
- The white extension overlapped with the frame (cols 43-47 overlaps with cols 44-47)
- Blue center was collected
Left blue square:
- Player at cols 20-23 (white at 20, green at 21-23)
- Frame at cols 24-27
- White is on the LEFT side of the green, pointing LEFT away from the frame
- SPACE extended white from col 20 to cols 16-20
- The white extension did NOT overlap with the frame (cols 16-20 doesn't overlap with cols 24-27)
- But the blue center was still collected!
Wait, so in the left blue square case, the white extension extended AWAY from the frame, but the blue center was still collected. This contradicts my earlier understanding.
Let me re-examine the left blue square case. Maybe I'm misremembering the positions.
Actually, let me look at the history more carefully. The left blue square frame is at cols 24-27, and the blue center is at cols 25-26 (inside the frame). The player is at cols 20-23, with white at col 20. The player is to the LEFT of the frame.
When SPACE is pressed, the white extends from col 20 to cols 16-20 (to the LEFT, away from the frame). The blue center at cols 25-26 moves to cols 17-18 (inside the white extension).
So the blue center "jumped" from inside the frame (cols 25-26) to inside the white extension (cols 17-18), even though the white extension extended away from the frame.
This suggests that the collection doesn't require the white extension to overlap with the frame. Instead, the collection happens when the player is adjacent to the frame and presses SPACE.
But in the current case, the player is adjacent to the frame (gap of 1 cell), and pressing SPACE didn't collect the blue center. Why?
Hmm, maybe the issue is that the white extension is already large (16 pixels). In the successful cases, the white extension was small (4 pixels) before pressing SPACE.
Let me check:
- Right blue square: white was small (4 pixels), SPACE extended to large (16 pixels), collected.
- Left blue square: white was small (4 pixels), SPACE extended to large (16 pixels), collected.
- Current: white is large (16 pixels), SPACE retracted to small (4 pixels), then extended back to large (16 pixels), but didn't collect.
Wait, but I pressed SPACE twice. The first SPACE retracted the white from large to small. The second SPACE extended the white from small to large. So the second SPACE should have collected the blue center, but it didn't.
Maybe the issue is that the white extension needs to be small AND the player needs to be in a specific position relative to the frame.
Let me try a different approach. Let me retract the white (press SPACE once), then move LEFT (if possible), then extend (press SPACE again).
Currently, the player is at cols 36-43, with white at cols 36-40 (large). If I press SPACE, the white retracts to col 40 (small, adjacent to the green at cols 41-43). Then I can try to move LEFT. The player would be at cols 32-39, but the frame is at cols 32-35, so the player might be blocked.
Actually, let me try pressing SPACE once to retract, then see if I can move LEFT.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# White is large (16 pixels), need to retract first
# Then try to move LEFT to get closer to the frame

result = action(['SPACE'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After SPACE (retract):")
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['color'] in ['N', 'W', 'b', 'c', 'g']:
        print(f"  id={node['id']}, color={node['color']}, pixels={node['pixels']}, boundary={node['boundary']}")

# Try to move LEFT after retracting
result2 = action(['LEFT'])
seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nAfter LEFT:")
for node in seg2['nodes']:
    if node['color'] in ['N', 'W', 'b', 'c', 'g']:
        print(f"  id={node['id']}, color={node['color']}, pixels={node['pixels']}, boundary={node['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
